content:
The mistakes I stopped making after rebuilding from scratch.

7 min read
Just now

--

There is a folder on my laptop that I never delete.
Not because the projects inside are successful.
Because they’re embarrassing.
Every few months, I open that folder and look at code I wrote in the past. It reminds me that programming isn’t about memorizing syntax. It’s about changing the way you think.
A few months ago, I opened one of my old Python automation projects. It was supposed to organize files, process CSV reports, and generate summaries automatically. When I originally finished it, I remember leaning back in my chair thinking,
“This is probably the cleanest code I’ve ever written.”
That confidence lasted exactly three minutes.
The first function I opened was nearly 250 lines long.
The same validation logic appeared in four different places.
Every file imported almost everything.
Configuration values were hardcoded.
Adding a single feature meant modifying five different files.
